---
title: "Exercise: Predicting Firm Exit with Logistic Regression"
description: "Practice logistic regression end-to-end: fit a model for binary firm exit, extend it with additional predictors, interpret odds ratios, compute scenario-based predicted probabilities, and communicate findings with inline R code."
author: "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
date: 2026-06-16
date-modified: 2026-06-16
image: ../../assets/images/r-programming.png

learning-objectives:
  - "Fit and extend a logistic regression model with `glm()` and compare specifications using AIC"
  - "Interpret a logistic regression coefficient as an odds ratio and communicate it in plain language"
  - "Compute scenario-based predicted probabilities for two contrasting firm profiles"
  - "Embed numerical results in written text using inline R code"

categories:
  - R
  - regression
  - statistics
  - MA
  - exercise

level:
  - MA

content-type: exercise

citation:
  type: entry
  container-title: "Methods Hub"
  title: "Exercise: Predicting Firm Exit with Logistic Regression"
  editor:
    - name: "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
  url: https://euf-methodology-hub.netlify.app/resources/r-programming/exercise-logistic-regression-firm-exit
---

::: {.callout-tip}
## Before you start
This exercise accompanies the [Logistic Regression for Binary Outcomes](logistic-regression-in-r.qmd) tutorial. Work through that page first.

**Data:** `bisnode-firms.csv` — ~17,500 Hungarian manufacturing and services firms. Download from [gabors-data-analysis.com](https://gabors-data-analysis.com/datasets/#bisnode-firms) and place in a `data/` subfolder.

**Business question:** *Can we predict which firms are likely to exit the market in the next year?*
:::

---

# Setup

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)
library(modelsummary)
library(marginaleffects)
library(broom)

firms <- read_csv("data/bisnode-firms.csv")
```

Take two minutes to explore the data:

```{r}
glimpse(firms)
```

**Before coding:** What does one row represent? What does `exit = 1` mean?

*Your answer:*

---

# Task 1: Fit a Logistic Regression Model

Fit a logistic regression model that predicts firm exit using:

- `sales_log` (log of sales revenue)
- `profit_margin` (profit as a share of sales)
- `employees_log` (log of number of employees)
- `age` (firm age in years)

Call your model `model_base`.

```{r}
# model_base <- ___
```

```{r}
# summary(model_base)
```

**1a.** Which predictors are statistically significant at the 5% level?

*Your answer:*

**1b.** Based on the signs of the coefficients, which factors increase and which decrease the probability of exit? Does this make economic sense?

*Your answer:*

---

# Task 2: Extend Your Model

Add at least **two more predictors** to your model. Use subject-matter intuition: which firm characteristics might predict exit?

```{r}
# names(firms)   # explore available variables
```

```{r}
# model_ext <- ___
```

Compare your two models with `modelsummary()`:

```{r}
# modelsummary(list("Base" = model_base, "Extended" = model_ext), ...)
```

**2a.** Did adding the new predictors improve model fit? Use AIC to compare (lower is better).

*Your answer:*

**2b.** Did any coefficients shift notably when you added the new variables? What might that indicate?

*Your answer:*

---

# Task 3: Interpret Coefficients as Odds Ratios

Compute and display the odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals for your extended model:

```{r}
# exp(coef(model_ext))
# exp(confint(model_ext))
```

**Task 3:** Choose **one predictor** and write a complete interpretation using this template:

> *"A one-unit increase in [variable] is associated with a multiplication of the odds of firm exit by [odds ratio]. This means the odds of exit [increase/decrease] by [X]%. This effect is [statistically significant / not significant] at the 5% level."*

*Your answer:*

---

# Task 4: Predicted Probabilities for Two Firm Profiles

Define two contrasting firm profiles and compute the predicted probability of exit for each:

```{r}
# scenarios <- tibble(
#   label         = c("Profile A: ___", "Profile B: ___"),
#   sales_log     = c(___, ___),
#   employees_log = c(___, ___),
#   profit_margin = c(___, ___),
#   age           = c(___, ___)
#   # add predictors you included in model_ext
# )

# scenarios |>
#   mutate(pred_prob = predict(model_ext, newdata = scenarios, type = "response"))
```

**Task 4:** Describe your two profiles and explain what story the predicted probabilities tell.

*Your answer:*

---

# Task 5: Communicate a Finding with Inline R Code

Write a short paragraph (3–5 sentences) summarising one key finding. Embed at least **two numbers as inline R code** (not typed manually).

*Your paragraph:*

---

# Bonus: Average Marginal Effects

Compare the AME for `profit_margin` from logistic regression to the LPM coefficient for the same variable. Are they similar? What does this tell you about when the LPM might be acceptable?

```{r}
# avg_slopes(model_ext)
# lpm <- lm(exit ~ sales_log + profit_margin + employees_log + age, data = firms)
```

*Your answer:*
